ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Chicken Masala; FOODON_00002670 (chicken dish) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific masala preparation code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept specifically covers Chicken Masala as a prepared dish; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Chicken Masala is approximately 300 g, comprising bone-in or boneless chicken pieces in a spiced tomato-based sauce, served with bread or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry (dry type) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Chicken Masala is a spiced dry-style chicken curry closely analogous to the ICMR-NIN 'chicken curry (dry type)' entry. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition). Turkish-style preparation uses similar spice profiles and cooking methods; the adaptation is considered defensible. USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed' (FDC ID 171477) was used as a cross-check for protein and fat values.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed chicken and spice dishes — analogous estimate based on chicken curry GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the closest analogous entry for chicken curry with tomato-based sauce, which is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish. The low GI reflects the dominant protein and fat content with minimal starchy carbohydrate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (30 × 12.6) / 100 ≈ 3.8, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.2 × 3 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~1260 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full restaurant serving can contribute over 50% of the recommended daily sodium intake of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) and low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Masala has a low glycemic index and low carbohydrate load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan bread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (~2.3 g per 100 g) and sodium (~420 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Chicken Masala provides lean protein and beneficial spices such as turmeric and cumin with anti-inflammatory properties, the moderate saturated fat and high sodium content in restaurant portions warrant mindful consumption for individuals managing cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Masala is a spiced chicken dish prepared by marinating chicken pieces in a blend of aromatic spices — including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, ginger, and garlic — then cooking them in a tomato-onion base with oil and yogurt to create a rich, deeply flavoured sauce. The dish originates from South Asian culinary tradition but has been widely adopted across Middle Eastern and Turkish cuisines, where it is served as a main course alongside flatbreads such as pide or lavash, or with rice. The texture is tender and juicy, with a complex spice profile that is warming and mildly pungent. Nutritionally, Chicken Masala is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-GI dietary patterns. The spice blend contributes antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compounds. The sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free and halal-compliant when prepared with permissible ingredients. At Sultan Palace in Singapore, it is served as a full restaurant portion priced at SGD 25.90.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken masala?
Is chicken masala good for diabetics?
What spices are in chicken masala?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Chicken Masala (Turkish style) exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chicken curry (dry type) entry, which is the closest defensible analogue. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous chicken curry entry. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' (2017), chicken curry dry type, as the closest approved-source analogue for Chicken Masala. Cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 for protein and fat plausibility. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous chicken curry data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Chicken Masala was found. Sodium value reflects typical restaurant-style preparation with added salt and spice pastes; home-prepared versions may have lower sodium. The dish is served at Sultan Palace (Boat Quay) in Singapore at SGD 25.90. Turkish-style Chicken Masala shares its spice profile with South Asian masala preparations but may incorporate regional spice variations such as baharat. Halal status assumed based on restaurant context (Turkish and Lebanese cuisine establishment); kosher status not confirmed. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN composite data for cooked spiced chicken dishes.
